background: A researcher aimed to investigate whether women who have taken the oral contraceptive pill experience menopause earlier or later than other women. He selected a group of women born in 1930, as they were young enough for some to have taken the pill but old enough for some to have reached menopause. Using a general practice's age-sex register, he identified 132 women born in 1930. Women who claimed to have experienced menopause were verified by measuring their follicle stimulating hormone (FSH) levels. Of the 132 women, 101 were excluded for various reasons, leaving a sample of 31 women. Among these, 12 had taken the pill, and 19 had not. The researcher concluded that taking the pill does not delay menopause.

task.query: What was the design of this study?
Is the sample of 31 women representative of the population of interest?
task.answer: If the population of interest is taken as all menopausal British women, then the representativeness of this general practice is relevant. We have no information about this, although it does not seem unreasonable to carry out this type of study in a single practice. All 132 women born in 1930 were investigated, so there was no selection bias. However, we are assuming that the register of patients is accurate and complete, and the fact that $21 / 132$ were not contactable casts some doubt on this. Of these 132 women, only 31 were actually studied, for the various reasons stated. Most of the exclusions are reasonable, although it is not clear why the unmarried women were excluded. The sample appears reasonably representative.

task.query: What was the fundamental error in the design of this study?
task.answer: The major problem with this study is that if the use of the pill delays the menopause then at the time of the study some women who will have had their menopause delayed will still have been premenopausal and so excluded from the study. The design does not allow the research objective to be investigated.

task.query: What design is needed to answer the question originally posed?
task.answer: This is a question that could be answered by a cohort study. If the researcher had taken, for example, all the women born in 1930 and waited until they had all reached the menopause, then he could make a valid comparison. However, it should be noted that it is not really good practice to lump together all pill users regardless of the length of pill use or the age at which it was taken. The cohort design would allow these factors to be investigated in relation to the age of menopause.

data:
\begin{tabular}{lccc}
\hline & & \multicolumn{2}{c}{ Age at menopause (years) } \
& $n$ & $n e a n$ & SD \
\hline Pill users & 12 & 47.2 & 2.1 \
Non pill users & 19 & 47.5 & 2.1 \
